branchingScenario.title: AN40.1-5 | Organs of hearing and equilibrium — Clinical Case

startScreen.startScreenSubtitle: A 6-year-old child presents with ear pain and hearing loss following recurrent otitis media. Work through this case exploring the anatomy of the ear, otoscopy technique, and the organs of hearing and equilibrium.

endScreens.endScreenSubtitle: You correctly identified ear anatomy, otoscopy technique, and the functional organisation of the inner ear.
You showed some understanding but missed key concepts. Review the ossicular chain, myringotomy site, and cochlea vs semicircular canal function.
Revisit EAM structure, otoscopy technique, middle ear ossicles, safe myringotomy quadrant, and inner ear anatomy.

params.text:
Case Presentation

A 6-year-old child is brought by his mother with right ear pain, fever, and reduced hearing for 3 days. On otoscopy, you see a bulging, erythematous tympanic membrane with a middle ear effusion.

The external auditory meatus is approximately 2.5 cm long in adults and comprises an outer cartilaginous one-third and an inner bony two-thirds.

To straighten the EAM for otoscopy in adults, the pinna is pulled upward and backward. In children under 3 years, it is pulled downward and backward due to the less developed bony canal.

branchingQuestion.question: The child needs a myringotomy to drain the middle ear effusion. In which quadrant of the tympanic membrane should the incision be made to avoid damaging the ossicles and chorda tympani nerve?

alternatives.text: Anteroinferior (or posteroinferior) quadrant — the safe quadrant
Posterosuperior quadrant where the membrane is thinnest

feedback.title: Not quite
feedback.subtitle: The posterosuperior quadrant is dangerous — the ossicles and chorda tympani nerve are located there. Myringotomy is safely performed in the anteroinferior quadrant.

alternatives.text: Directly through the centre (umbo) for best drainage

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: The umbo is where the malleus handle attaches — incising here would damage the ossicle. The safe site for myringotomy is the anteroinferior quadrant.

params.text:
Further Investigation

Correct! The anteroinferior quadrant is the safe site for myringotomy.

An audiogram shows conductive hearing loss. You review the middle ear ossicular chain: the three ossicles are the malleus (attached to the tympanic membrane), the incus, and the stapes (attached to the oval window). These transmit and amplify sound vibrations from the tympanic membrane to the inner ear.

In the inner ear, the cochlea is the organ of hearing, while the semicircular canals detect rotational acceleration for equilibrium.

branchingQuestion.question: After treatment, the child develops vertigo. Which inner ear structures are responsible for detecting rotational acceleration (equilibrium)?

alternatives.text: The three semicircular canals detect rotational acceleration
The cochlea detects both sound and rotational movement

feedback.title: Not quite
feedback.subtitle: The cochlea is specifically for hearing. The semicircular canals (three loop-shaped structures) are the organs that detect rotational acceleration for equilibrium.

alternatives.text: The middle ear ossicles sense equilibrium through vibration

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: The ossicles transmit sound, not equilibrium signals. Rotational acceleration is detected by the three semicircular canals of the inner ear.

params.text:
Clinical Synthesis

Excellent! Key ear anatomy points:

  • EAM: ~2.5 cm long; outer cartilaginous 1/3, inner bony 2/3
  • Otoscopy: Adults — pull pinna up and back; Children <3 — pull down and back
  • Ossicles: Malleus → incus → stapes (TM to oval window)
  • Myringotomy: Anteroinferior quadrant (safe site)
  • Cochlea: Hearing; Semicircular canals: Rotational equilibrium
